---
title: "Control Chart Concepts"
categories: [statistics, procedure, quality]
tier-focus: procedure
related:
  - ../../wiki/concepts/control-charts.md
  - ../../wiki/concepts/process-capability.md
  - ../../wiki/r-code/control-charts-r.md
---

# Control Chart Concepts

## Learning Goals

1. Explain the purpose of a control chart and the difference between common and special cause variation
2. Choose the correct chart type for a given situation
3. Apply the Western Electric run rules to detect out-of-control signals
4. Distinguish control limits from specification limits

## In Plain English

A control chart monitors a process over time to detect **special cause variation** (unexpected shifts, trends, or spikes) against the background of **common cause variation** (natural process noise).

Control limits are set at **±3σ** from the process center — not at specification limits. A point outside control limits signals a process change worth investigating.

## Chart Selection Guide

| Data type | Subgroup size | Chart |
|-----------|--------------|-------|
| Measurements (continuous) | n ≥ 2 | X̄/R (n ≤ 10) or X̄/S (n > 10) |
| Fraction defective | Variable | p-chart |
| Count of defects (fixed area) | Fixed | c-chart |
| Defects per unit (variable area) | Variable | u-chart |

## Control Limits Formulas

**X̄/R chart:**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R}, \quad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\bar{R}$$

$$UCL_R = D_4\bar{R}, \quad LCL_R = D_3\bar{R}$$

where A₂, D₃, D₄ are control chart constants from the table (depend on n).

**p-chart:**

$$UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}, \quad LCL_p = \max\!\left(0,\; \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}\right)$$

## Western Electric Run Rules

A process is out of control if **any** of the following occur:

1. One point beyond ±3σ
2. Two of three consecutive points beyond ±2σ (same side)
3. Four of five consecutive points beyond ±1σ (same side)
4. Eight consecutive points on the same side of the center line

## Control Limits vs. Specification Limits

| | Control Limits | Specification Limits |
|--|----------------|---------------------|
| Set by | Process data (±3σ) | Engineering / customer |
| Purpose | Detect process changes | Define acceptable product |
| Relationship | A capable process has CL inside spec limits | |

> A process can be **in control** but **not capable** if its natural variation exceeds the spec limits.

## Related Wiki Pages

- [Control Charts](../../wiki/concepts/control-charts.md)
- [Process Capability](../../wiki/concepts/process-capability.md)
- [R: Control Charts](../../wiki/r-code/control-charts-r.md)
- [Control Chart Examples](../../wiki/examples/control-chart-examples.md)
